sections.title: General Guidance
sections.content: Burns can damage the skin and deeper tissues. Quick, correct first aid reduces pain, infection, and long-term damage.

sections.title: Call Emergency Services Immediately If
items: Burn is larger than the person's palm
Burn involves face, hands, feet, genitals, or major joints

steps.title: Stop Burning Process
steps.subtitle: Remove from heat source, Stop-Drop-Roll if clothing is on fire. Turn off electricity before touching a person with electrical burns.

steps.title: Assess Burn Severity
steps.subtitle:
Determine depth (1st, 2nd, 3rd degree) and percentage of body affected.

First-Degree (Superficial):
  • Red, painful, dry skin
  • No blisters
  • Example: mild sunburn

Second-Degree (Partial Thickness):
  • Red or blotchy
  • Blisters
  • Swelling and severe pain

Third-Degree (Full Thickness) — Medical Emergency:
  • White, charred, brown, or leathery skin
  • May be painless (nerve damage)
  • Requires urgent medical care

steps.title: Cool the Burn
steps.subtitle: Run cool (not ice-cold) water over the burn for 10–20 minutes. Do NOT use ice (can cause more tissue damage).

steps.title: Remove Tight Items
steps.subtitle: Gently remove rings, watches, or tight clothing before swelling starts. Do NOT remove stuck clothing.

steps.title: Protect the Burn
steps.subtitle:
Cover loosely with a sterile, non-stick dressing or clean cloth. Do NOT break blisters.
Do NOT use ice, butter, ointments, toothpaste, or creams.

steps.title: Pain Management
steps.subtitle: Provide over-the-counter pain relievers (if appropriate). Monitor for shock, keep the area elevated if possible.
